title: Iodine on Silk — Time-Critical Light-Activated Treatment
stainChemistry: Iodine on silk forms charge-transfer complexes with silk protein's amino acid side chains (particularly lysine and histidine residues). Unlike cellulose fibers, silk's protein structure is susceptible to strong oxidizers. The I₂ molecules bond through van der Waals forces and some coordinate bonding to the protein. Iodine on silk is time-sensitive — exposure to light accelerates iodine oxidation to brown iodine oxides, permanently fixing the stain to the silk protein.
whyThisWorks: Sodium thiosulfate reduction works on silk exactly as on cellulose (I₂ → I⁻), but timing is absolutely critical. The treated area must be protected from light to prevent light-induced oxidation of residual iodine. We use minimal hydrogen peroxide (3%, very brief contact) rather than the more aggressive treatment used on cellulose. The key is speed and light-avoidance, not aggressive chemistry.

spottingProtocol.agent: sodium thiosulfate solution (1 oz hypo per 16 oz distilled water, room temperature)
spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting bottle, dark cloth or room
spottingProtocol.instruction: Sponge the thiosulfate solution gently into the stain from the back side. Work quickly and keep the treated area COVERED from light (use a dark cloth). Do NOT leave the garment in sunlight or bright light. Speed and darkness are more important than extended dwell time on silk. Sponge 2-3 times and move to the next step.

spottingProtocol.agent: hydrogen peroxide 3% — minimal strength and time
spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting bottle, dark cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Use hydrogen peroxide 3% only if brown iodine oxide residue is visible. Apply briefly (30-60 seconds maximum) and keep the area covered from light. Do NOT use peroxide on silk unless absolutely necessary — it can weaken protein fibers. Bleaching damage on silk is often irreversible.

Rinse gently with cool distilled water to remove thiosulfate residue. Keep the garment covered from light during and after rinsing. Use as little mechanical action as possible. Silk protein can be damaged by repeated friction.

spottingProtocol.agent: distilled water (final rinse only — no acids on silk protein)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Final gentle sponge with distilled water. Do NOT use acetic acid on silk protein — the acid can cause hydrolysis and weaken the fibers. Allow excess water to drip into the under-pad and do not wring. Keep covered from light.

escalation: If iodine stain persists after sodium thiosulfate treatment, DO NOT use stronger chemicals or repeat hydrogen peroxide. Silk protein damage occurs easily. The stain may be permanent. Professional dry-clean solvent treatment is the only safe next step, but even that carries risk of silk damage. Consider the garment's value before proceeding.
commonMistakes: Using chlorine bleach — NEVER on silk, iodine becomes darker and fixes permanently.
Exposing treated area to sunlight or bright light — light accelerates iodine oxidation.
Using hot water — heat accelerates both oxidation and protein damage.
Scrubbing or rubbing the area — silk is weaker than cellulose and over-handling causes permanent damage.
scienceNote: The light-sensitivity of iodine stains on silk is a critical learning point. Iodine can be reduced chemically, but if light reaches the reduced iodine before you neutralize it, the iodine re-oxidizes to brown oxides (I₂O₄, I₂O₅) which bond irreversibly to silk protein. This is why professional spotters work quickly with treated silk and keep it covered. Understanding the photo-oxidation of iodine is essential to silk spotting.
safety: CRITICAL: Never use chlorine bleach on silk — it will permanently set the stain and damage the protein. Sodium thiosulfate is safe. Hydrogen peroxide 3% is minimally safe (brief contact only). Do NOT use strong oxidizers on silk protein. Standard spotting gloves required. Test all chemicals on an interior seam first.
materialWarnings: ALWAYS test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — silk dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
Avoid heat, avoid strong oxidizers, avoid mechanical stress.
Iodine on silk is TIME-CRITICAL — the longer the stain sits, the higher the risk of light-induced oxidation making it permanent.
Never heat-set an iodine stain on silk.
Chlorine bleach will permanently damage the fiber.
Excessive hydrogen peroxide can weaken silk protein.
Repeated spotting attempts will damage the fabric.
callPro: For valuable silk garments (silk blouses, scarves, specialty fabrics), professional dry cleaner with silk expertise is strongly recommended. DIY iodine spotting on silk carries high risk of fiber damage. If the garment is antique or delicate silk, do not attempt — professional only.
customerExplanation: Iodine on silk requires special care because silk is a protein fiber vulnerable to damage from strong chemicals and light. We applied sodium thiosulfate to reduce the iodine to a soluble form, worked quickly to prevent light from re-oxidizing it, and kept the garment covered from light. We minimized the use of hydrogen peroxide to protect the silk protein. The garment must be kept away from direct sunlight for 24 hours to prevent the stain from returning.

homeSolutions.agent: Silk + iodine: speed and darkness matter more than chemistry
homeSolutions.instruction: Iodine on silk is a narrow home window. Fresh iodine can evaporate and partially absorb into a blotting cloth BEFORE it fully bonds to silk fibroin — but only if you act within minutes AND keep the garment covered from light (light re-oxidizes iodine into more stable stains). If the stain is already set or dried, home options are thin — silk bans most of the tools that would work. Home best-case: fresh and light; anything else, pro.

Silk: no enzymes, no hot water, no alkaline, no alcohol (strips silk dye), no scrubbing or wringing. Iodine-specific: sodium thiosulfate is the correct chemistry but at home it's hard to control dwell/concentration on silk. The home tools allowed: cold water, cold milk (protein in milk binds some iodine), very dilute sodium thiosulfate ONLY if you have photography hypo on hand and can keep dwell under 2 minutes.

homeSolutions.agent: Blot fast — then keep the garment OUT of light
homeSolutions.instruction: If the iodine is fresh (under 5 minutes), press a dry white cloth straight down on the stain. Lift, move to a fresh section, press again. Light dabs — do not rub, do not press deep, do not apply any liquid yet. After blotting, immediately drape the garment with a dry white cloth or bag it in opaque material. Sunlight and even ambient light continue to oxidize iodine toward permanent brown; darkness buys you more time.

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test + cold milk soak (fresh stains only)
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab cool water on an inside seam, wait 60 seconds, blot. If clean, pour cold (fridge-cold) milk into a clean bowl and submerge just the stained area. Keep the bowl covered or in a dark cupboard for 10 minutes. Milk protein binds some iodine. Remove, rinse gently in cool water from the BACK. Blot dry. Unreliable but silk-safe — effectiveness varies by how fresh the stain is.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Use whole milk, not skim — more protein. Pour milk, do not dilute.

homeSolutions.agent: Dilute sodium thiosulfate IF you have it — STRICT 2-min ceiling on silk
homeSolutions.instruction: If you happen to have sodium thiosulfate (photography hypo or pool chlorine neutralizer): mix ¼ teaspoon in 1 cup of cool water. Dab with a cotton swab for 60 SECONDS, rinse with cool water from the back, and check. Max two applications, never more than 2 minutes total exposure. Most households won't have thiosulfate — that's fine, escalate instead of substituting something harsher.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix sodium thiosulfate with bleach or strong acid. Never leave on silk past 2 minutes total.

homeSolutions.agent: Press dry + lay flat — never hang wet silk
homeSolutions.instruction: Press between dry white towels. No wringing, no twisting. Lay flat on a fresh dry towel, reshape while damp. Air-dry at room temperature OUT OF DIRECT LIGHT for the first 24 hours — even residual iodine traces can re-darken in strong light. No heat source.

If the milk soak didn't clear the stain, thiosulfate isn't available, or a shadow remains, take the garment to a silk specialist (not every dry cleaner — call ahead). Tell them: "Iodine on silk, age [x]. Treated at home with cold blot + [milk soak / nothing / dilute thiosulfate]. Kept covered from light. No alcohol, no enzymes, no heat, no bleach." Pros have controlled-concentration thiosulfate and the silk-safe technique to apply it without fiber damage.

meta.sourceRef: Expert Garment Restoration & Stain Removal Tips

products.name: Sodium Thiosulfate (Hypo Crystals)
products.note: Use minimal quantities on silk. Essential for iodine reduction, but speed and light protection are more important than extended treatment time.

aftercare: Apply only a light dusting of sodium thiosulfate powder to the iodine stain. Do NOT work it into the silk fibers vigorously — silk protein damage occurs with mechanical action. Let the powder sit briefly in a dark room or under a cloth cover. This is a preliminary step only.
